title: Part 4
text: were quite similar. In fact, there are more than a few parallels between these two crucial thinkers, pushed to the margins of the social sciences in much the same way. Sociologists now read Tarde but will not be able to place van Gennep; anthropologists know a bit about van Gennep but are unable to place Tarde – another sign of the institutional split between folklore/ ethnography and sociology and the almost total lack of interdisciplinary dialogue within branches of the social sciences which were once part and parcel of the same larger field. Even scholars of Marcel Mauss, including Marcel Fournier (2006), have paid surprisingly little attention to the important overlaps between the work of Mauss and van Gennep as two complementary and highly original thinkers. The clear tendency in the literature on Mauss is to see van Gennep as an opponent to all the Durkheimians, evidently – and centrally – including Mauss. As will be discussed below, this is only partially true. Mauss scholars, for example, uncritically take Mauss’ very negative 1910 review of Rites of Passage at face value, and use it as a proof that Mauss, just like Durkheim, held van Gennep’s work in very low regard. They omit to discuss how Mauss’ post-World War I writings actually moved closer and closer to the original positions of van Gennep, and further and further away from Durkheim. This is of course not something Mauss would ever say explicitly, but it shows from the substance of his later writings: his notion of habitus, his insistence on bodily techniques, his comparative dimension which in his work on personhood re-incorporated a historical-genealogical dimension, his close and insistent attention to etymology, his re-engagement with psychology, his relational stance on social phenomena, even his belated understanding of the political peril embedded in Durkheim’s collectivist creed – all of these developments in Mauss’ oeuvre bring him in close contact with van Gennep. While recognizing that van Gennep did eventually make a shift in orientation from ethnography and sociology toward French folklore, we need to engage with Arnold van Gennep’s life’s work and its foundational value for the social sciences, before putting him into boxes, burying him in disciplinary quarrels or reducing Liminality and the Modern 30 his efforts to an embryonic beginning of whatever -ism. We need to bring the potential of his academic project to life again, in order to be able to build on to the framework he left behind. This is the aim of the first two chapters of this book. Missing Rites of Passage: The Early Life and Work of Charles-Arnold Kurr van Gennep The man we today talk about as Arnold van Gennep was born as Charles-Arnold Kurr van Gennep on April 23 in 1873 in Ludwigsburg, Würtemberg. His father was lieutenant at the court of Würtemberg, which was then an independent kingdom (another genius, Einstein, was born in nearby Ulm in 1879, the same year van Gennep left the kingdom). His mother was of Dutch origin. His father descended from French migrants. Both French and Dutch were spoken at home. The fact that he grew up in a tri-lingual environment and within a migrant family would later serve him well. At the age of 8 he was, according to Ketty van Gennep (1964: 9) fluent in reading and speaking French, German and Dutch. When he was six years old his parents separated (due to a certain ‘incompabilité d’humeur’, as his daughter would diplomatically state it, likely repeating an expression she had heard from her father; ibid.: 4), and van Gennep moved with his mother to live in Lyons, France. From what can be reconstructed, he lost all contact with his biological father. There can be little doubt that this sudden and radical separation from his father and his childhood environment left deep traces in his personality. Arnold van Gennep: A Cosmopolitan Frenchman In the various encyclopaedia entries that exist, Arnold van Gennep is variously introduced as a ‘Belgian’, ‘Dutch’ or ‘German’ anthropologist or folklorist. While van Gennep’s mother was indeed ‘Dutch’, and while he was in a formal sense born in a Germanic state, the truth is that Van Gennep was French before all else. He moved to France at the age of six, and except shorter stays abroad (including fieldwork periods in Algeria and his three year spell at Neuchâtel in Switzerland) he studied and lived in France the rest of his life. French also became his language of intimacy. This, however, would not prevent Durkheim from questioning his Frenchness when van Gennep during World War I tried, once again, to get his foot inside the French academic job market (more on which in Chapter 2). In 1884 Van Gennep’s mother married a French doctor, Paul Raugé, who had a summer practice in the province of Savoy. Van Gennep’s strong attachment to this region, which he would come to consider his adopted homeland, dates from his early youth. He loved Savoy. He was to travel through Savoy, village by village, collecting ethnographic and folkloric materials, learning the dialects, rituals and traditions of each single village. This material would make up some of the data later to be used in his monumental work on French folklore. Arnold van Gennep: Fragments of a Life-Work at the Thresholds 31 However, if van Gennep was in most ways simply French, it is also true that his background made him quite different from most other French and Parisian intellectuals, with whom he would come into contact. He did not really belong anywhere, and he was difficult to categorize – then as now. He possessed knowledge that would surprise everyone. He had read things that no other intellectual in France knew off. Van Gennep’s sublime linguistic skills meant that he more than any other person working within ethnology and anthropology in the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries gathered and read literature from all corners of Europe. This alone allowed him to make a real contribution to French anthropology and sociology, as he reviewed and translated much of this European literature. Arnold van Gennep probably was not what one would call an ‘easy person’. From his early youth he gained a reputation for doing things his own way. He initially enrolled in a school in Lyons, but due to numerous behavioural problems he was transferred to Sainte-Barbe-des Champs in Paris in 1884. He completed high school in Nice, receiving prizes for his grades, but also penalties for frequent misconduct. During his time in Nice he learned English, and also took lessons in Italian and Spanish. He then taught himself Portuguese. He excelled in most subjects, except mathematics, for which he never developed a passion. In 1892, van Gennep received the philo aux sciences restreintes degree from a lycée in Grenoble. His adoptive father pressured him to study surgery, but Van Gennep refused, and eventually chose to start a career in diplomacy. Van Gennep then moved to Paris. He decided that the Sorbonne did not offer what he was looking for and enrolled at l’Ecole des langues orientales to study Arabic and at l’École pratique des Hautes Études, to study general linguistics and Egyptology. He also enrolled for lectures at the section of sciences religieuses in that same school, where he studied primitive religion and Islamic culture. Among others, he here came into contact with the great linguist, Antoine Meillet, a member of the Durkheim circle, and got to know Marcel Mauss as well. Mauss was just one year older than van Gennep, and they shared identical interests. In this period, van Gennep published his first articles on numismatics. His very first publication dates from 1894: a two page commentary on Merovingian coins in Revue Numismatique. He had been collecting coins from his early youth, a passion he retained throughout his life. His publications from 1894–1897 were all on numismatics. Van Gennep had also developed a strong interest in animal branding and property marks since his stays in Savoy. These interests brought him into contact with ethnography. He started to develop very ambitious academic plans, seeking out a synthesis between linguistics, ethnography and ancient history. As he wrote in 1895, at the age of 22, and still an under-graduate, his goal would be to ‘revise theories on taboo and take up again, on completely new foundations, the question of the origin of languages and alphabets’. 5 When he 5 In his own words, ‘reviser toute la théorie du tabou; à reprendre sur des bases nouvelles la question de l’origine des écritures et des alphabets [...] l’idée que la marque crée un lieu d’appartenance et de parenté; à examiner quelle est la situation exacte, par Liminality and the Modern 32 assembled his academic curriculum for a job application in 1911, he restated the centrality of property marks and signs, as such a study would allow him to ‘revise the whole theory of taboo; to reconstruct the entire question of the origin of writing and alphabets on new foundations; to conduct research into the exact meaning of tattooing, body painting, and all mutilations implying that such marks create a membership and kinship bond’ (as quoted and translated by Belmont 1979: 105). We must appreciate van Gennep’s interest in numismatics and in property marks as much more than a mere anecdote and for reasons that can be spelled out from the outset: essentially, the ‘stamping’ of liquid forms into a ‘hard’ or ‘frozen’ still-image is of course very basically what happens with coinage and with the invention of alphabetical writing; but such techniques have their close parallels in social processes of ‘stamping’ liminal experiences onto the neophyte and his or her cohort, producing lasting forms, and closing the liminal period. It is of utmost importance that Weber, in his genealogical approach, emphatically used the concept of ‘stamping’ (prägung) to designate the process whereby certain ideas or practices, initially identifiable within specific social groups, become distilled and established as lasting forms within wider populations. The question that van Gennep somehow intuited from an early age concerns how ritual and symbolic forms can leave lasting marks on both individuals and societies undergoing such ritual experiences. The ‘marque’ stands as the physical, concrete sign that centrally links the spheres of religion and taboo, belonging and kinship, language and writing. And rituals are played out in the tension between form and content. Marriage, Flight from France and Return to Paris The situation was this: Arnold van Gennep was an extremely talented and productive young man finding himself in Paris in a period where ideas and entire university disciplines were in the making. He graduated in 1896 at the Paris School of Oriental Languages. He had also kicked off his career as translator. His first two translations were an article on Islamic law from Dutch to French, published in the prestigious Revue de l’Histoire des Religions, and Frazer’s immensely influential book on totemism, both of which were printed in 1898. 6 rapport aux langues proprement dites ou communes, des langues specials’.As quoted in the short and precise biography posted online by the Musée d’ethnographie Neuchâtel at http://www.men.ch/infomusee.asp/1-3-418-99-1520-99-5-4-1 (last accessed May 4 2013). Arnold van Gennep made a lasting impact at the ethnographic museum at Neuchâtel which he led from 1912–1915. 6 Durkheim had himself hoped to be in charge of that important translation, the reference work over all others for any comparative discussion on totemism and religion. In spring 1898, while Mauss was in London to study the British school of anthropology, Durkheim asked him to approach Frazer with this translation in mind – but too late, for the young van Gennep had already been offered the job (with A. Dirr). On May 10, 1898, Durkheim writes, seemingly rather annoyed, to Mauss: ‘My project to have Totemism translated has gone down the drain. [...] You can tell Frazer that my intention was to have it Arnold van Gennep: Fragments of a Life-Work at the Thresholds 33 Translation and non-academic publication would remain van Gennep’s main source of income for much of his life. It was Arnold van Gennep who would later translate classics such as Westermarck into French; he also translated the eighteen-volume-long series of Havelock Ellis’ works on sexual psychology, with volume I appearing in 1908. He had met Frazer, and was building up a huge knowledge of European anthropology, developing his ideas with and against the French and British schools, but with a keen eye to developments in Germany and the rest of Europe. He had started to publish and was following lectures in some of France’s best departments, performing brilliantly. And then he left. Shortly after his marriage in 1897, he applied for a teaching position at a Polish lycée, in Czentochowa, as a French teacher. The two events were connected and certainly go some way toward explaining van Gennep’s ‘confession’ regarding the significance of Rites of Passage: it seems certain that the ‘ten years of darkness’ for van Gennep referred to both his personal and professional life as it developed from around 1897 to 1908, when the book was finished. Van Gennep’s marriage in 1897 led to a painful break with his mother and his adoptive father, who could not accept the social standing of his chosen one. His wife was young and, judging from the record, very beautiful indeed, but she was also extremely poor, and brought no dowry whatsoever to the marriage. In this as in so many other cases, van Gennep chose to disregard social expectations. He married her because he wanted to. Van Gennep would not really reconcile with his parents again: the break was apparently final, and this certainly must have added to a certain feeling of isolation and loneliness that accompanied his life. The wedding itself was largely devoid of that crucial social recognition by close friends and family that lend rituals of reintegration their significance. 1897 was also the year that van Gennep moved to Russian Poland, attempting to start a career as a school teacher. It was another travel into unknown lands, marital solitude and uncertain futures. It is not entirely clear whether van Gennep had ever been formally adopted by Paul Raugé; and here again the myriad of official encyclopaedic entries are at odds with each other. It seems likely, for it was common custom for men to adopt the children of re-married women, and it is beyond doubt that Paul Raugé to some extent did come to function as Arnold’s father. What we do know with certainty is that up until 1897 van Gennep signed five of his publications as ‘Arnold Raugé van Gennep’ (Ketty van Gennep 1964: 15–16), a clear sign that he recognized Paul as his father, also in the formal sense. After 1897 he would never do so again, 7 nor would he turn to his biological father’s name: from then onwards he would simply call himself ‘Arnold van Gennep’. It is therefore what we rightly call him today. translated’ (Durkheim 1998: 136–7, my translation). There is some likelihood that this is the first time ever that Durkheim stumbled upon the name of Arnold van Gennep (in the letter to Mauss he refers to the ‘two translators’ of Frazer without mentioning their names). If so the ‘relationship’ between Durkheim and van Gennep started out on a somewhat negative note. 7 Ketty van Gennep, in her introduction to the Bibliographie, still bespeaks Paul Raugé as her grandfather. Liminality and the Modern 34 However, we also know that van Gennep continued to hold Paul Raugé in esteem, and there is at least one direct proof of that: his 1906 book, Mythes et Légendes, is dedicated to ‘Dr Paul Raugé’. Why did van Gennep leave France? He was in need of a job; simply to keep on studying was no longer an option. Because of his language skills he was well- qualified to serve France abroad. In Czentochowa he taught himself Russian in eight months. In the examination process for becoming titulaire, he translated from both Latin and Greek into Russian. He stayed there for four years, and published nothing in the period. However, if this job had taken him away from the centre of academic life in France and Europe, he did not exactly waste his time. 8 He taught himself Polish as well, and gained reading efficiency in several other Slavic languages. He read whatever he could get hold off in Slavic languages on myth and religion. He also travelled as much as he could, accompanied by his wife. Rites of Passage contains several references to readings and travels he made in this period. In Czentochowa he was surrounded by an air of suspicion. The French journals he received were checked by the authorities. Europe was a continent of suspicion in the early twentieth century, especially in disputed border areas like Russian Poland. When van Gennep much later, after World War I, developed his own approach to the study of nationalism (van Gennep 1922), it was certainly also as a reflection of his own life experiences within and across Europe’s national boundaries. Van Gennep returned to Paris with his wife in 1901. As indicated by the memoirs of Ketty van Gennep, the return was mainly due to his wife’s pregnancy and precarious health. The return to France would tragically coincide with the loss of their first child. They could not afford good hospitals. Upon his return, and from 1901 to 1908, he was employed as head of translations at the French ministry of Agriculture. His linguistic skills were by then second to none, and in a way that was always going to be his salvation. He practically mastered all Germanic, Latin and Slavic languages. He did not speak the Scandinavian languages, but could read them. And he engaged further with the ‘Oriental languages’ as well. In an article from 1927, discussing the use of the subconscious in the study of living languages, he mentions having mastered eighteen languages plus a number of their dialects (Belmont 1979: 7). 9 8 Without overstressing the parallel, it is worth noting how another French intellectual later in the twentieth century similarly left Paris and France in order to take up peripheral teaching and administrative positions for the French state in the far North, who lived for a shorter period in Poland (and later North Africa) and who also managed to do decisive research next to holding full-time administrative jobs – and whose surgeon father had mounted pressure on his son to become a doctor. Both would eventually drop the part of their name that made reference to their father. Both would return to the heart of French academia and eventually apply for a position at the Collège de France. Charles-Arnold became Arnold and Paul-Michel became Michel. The difference is that Foucault got the job. 9 As discussed by Belmont (1979: 7–9), in this same article van Gennep presents an indeed remarkable homemade theory of language learning. The first rule is to focus on the sounds and rhythms of a language, training the ear, and then exercising one’s muscles in Arnold van Gennep: Fragments of a Life-Work at the Thresholds 35 In 1903 his wife was pregnant again, and afraid to lose the child for a second time, the couple decided to move out of the city, to quieter Clamart just outside Paris. It meant a lot of commuting by train for van Gennep. A few years later the family would try to return to Paris, but as their young daughter, Ketty, got seriously ill with pleurisy their doctor advised them to return to the province. They did, but this time to Bourg-la-Reine, where train connections to the Ministry of Agriculture were better. Van Gennep would live most of his remaining life in Bourg-la-Reine; and here both he and his wife would die. There is a small street named after him just two blocks off the train station from where he commuted, Rue Arnold van Gennep. It is the only street, square or monument in the world stamped with his name. He did leave his mark somewhere, after all, even if one has to search quite hard for it. Paris and the Encounter with the French Social Sciences Van Gennep’s formal career now took place outside academia. Next to his ministerial position, he kept on translating and editing. For over thirty years, from 1906 to 1939, he edited, for a minor salary, the section Ethnographie-Folklore-Religions-Prehistoire in Mercure de France. However, upon his return to France, he still managed to advance his academic work. He was in fact amazingly productive considering the fact that he kept performing full- time as head of a ministry office. He also returned to his studies. Van Gennep now enrolled to study sciences religieuses with Léon Marillier at the École Pratique. He was in fine company once again, finer than ever, becoming part of that handful of young people who would later become Durkheim’s and Mauss’ collaborators: Mauss himself, Henri Hubert, Paul Fauconnet and many others. There is no doubt that Mauss and van Gennep were equally influenced by Marillier, and both took much inspiration from his insistence on a factually based social science, paying close attention to both detail and context, and never allowing ideas or theories to lift them away from the empirical ground. Upon Marillier’s sudden death in 1901, Marcel Mauss became van Gennep’s teacher and mentor, and therefore closely followed van Gennep in the work towards his first book. In 1903, Marcel Mauss proofread and thoroughly commented upon van Gennep’s thesis on taboo and totemism in Madagascar. Van Gennep’s interests during the first decade of the century would develop alongside those trying to imitate sounds and intonations, before moving on to the grammar, here starting out with the absolutely invariable elements and then progressing to the variable ones. Words, then, must be learned, not from their meaning, but from their root; in this way, any word summoned brings with it the whole linguistic cluster to which it belongs, whereas any direct reference to the meaning in the appropriation of a word will close the learner off from the larger semantic matrix from which that meaning emerges. One discerns, behind these rules, much of van Gennep’s ethnographic method. In fact, for him it was probably more or less the same thing. Liminality and the Modern 36 of the Durkheimians. This included the classical topics of totemism, taboo, the origins and nature of religion, magic, classification systems and the relationship between myth and ritual. In 1904 he succeeded where Mauss would constantly fail: he published his thesis in 1904 as Tabou et Totémisme à Madagascar. Étude Descriptive et Théorique. The book is dedicated to the memory of Léon Marillier. In the preface van Gennep reserves the final thanks to ‘mon ami Marcel Mauss’ (van Gennep 1904: 2). In this his first book, van Gennep approached two key questions of contemporary anthropology: taboos and totemic belief systems. Van Gennep here analysed totemism as a social system of classification, taking its role far beyond the more narrow field of religion. His understanding of Frazer’s merits and limits were already clearly formulated here. In 1906 he published his second book, Mythes et légendes d’Australie. It is one of the first books of the century openly to denounce analytical ethnocentrism and it was here also, as will be discussed in Chapter 2, that van Gennep began openly to expose the most blatant flaws in Durkheim’s work. Van Gennep had in the same period started to publish a flow of journal articles, all the while translating major works from English into French, most of which are still in use today. He published in French, Flemish, German and English, and in a wide plethora of journals. While van Gennep would continue to publish on animal branding, linguistics and more narrowly defined folkloric topics (he was a frequent contributor to Revues des Traditions Populaires), he would also start to publish material of a broader comparative relevance, as was the case for many of his articles published in Revues des Idées. A 1908 article in that journal, to give just one example, was entitled, Is there progress in civilization?. His answer was, as always, a thoughtful and reflective one, anticipating later critiques of evolutionism, ethnocentrism and scientific positivism by decades. A few shorter articles and commentaries also appeared in Man (the first in 1907). He finished Rites of Passage by the end of 1908, seeing it through to publication in 1909. Rites of Passage: The Book and What it Says Rites of Passage is a relatively short book. It is also an elegant book, analytically sophisticated yet clear-cut, and constantly exemplified with references to a rich variety of ethnographic case studies described with brief but engaged detail. Van Gennep built his argument on the available ethnographic data, comparing ritual forms at all ‘levels of development’, without reducing them all to one universal function. Van Gennep here, as elsewhere, showed an intimacy with the theoretical and empirical literature on religion and society in the major European languages, including French, German, English, Russian, Italian, Polish and Flemish. Van Gennep starts out by suggesting a meaningful classification of all existing rites. He distinguishes between rites that mark the passage of an individual or social group from one status to another from those which mark transitions in the Arnold van Gennep: Fragments of a Life-Work at the Thresholds 37 passage of time (e.g. harvest, New Year), whereupon he goes on to explore ‘the basis of characteristic patterns in the order of ceremonies’ (1960: 10). Stressing the importance of transitions in any society, van Gennep then singles out rites of passage as a special category, consisting of three sub-categories, namely rites of separation, transition rites, and rites of incorporation. Van Gennep suggests calling the middle stage in a rite of passage a liminal period (ibid.: 11). He calls transition rites liminal rites, and the rites of incorporation postliminal rites. Van Gennep further explains that even though the tripartite structure is always recognizable and to some extent present, the rites of separation, transition and incorporation are not always equally important or equally elaborated in specific rituals. He also noted that the tripartite structure is sometimes reduplicated in the transitional period itself: in liminality proper, the sequence of separation, transition and incorporation is often present. The opening chapter is followed by a chapter on the spatial dimension of ritual passages. As we will return to below, van Gennep held the spatial dimension of thresholds, doors, gateways and other transit zones to be fundamental for the cultural elaboration of ritual transitions and cultural transformation. Chapter 3 is called Individuals and Groups. Besides opening up the society/individual question (a question dear to van Gennep, and one that made him an enemy of Durkheimian collectivism), the chapter is in fact a discussion of gift exchange as foundational for social bonds. The rest of the book follows an extremely simple structure, equivalent to a life cycle: pregnancy, birth, initiation, marriage, funeral. In other words, the book elegantly takes the narrative form of a life-cycle exactly as it describes the rituals by which such a life-cycle is made socially significant and meaningful within the variety of world cultures. Van Gennep’s later multi-volume work on French ethnology would be guided by a very similar structure, moving from conception and birth rites to death and funeral rites. 10 It is also for this reason that we can safely state that Rites of Passage was van Gennep’s most important work, and a ‘breakthrough’. It in many ways served to organize everything else he did. The book ends with a chapter that includes a variety of ‘other rituals’ that do not easily fit neatly into the life cycle structure (but nonetheless confirms the tripartite structure) before it concludes by stressing the universality of the whole argument. It would be wrong to accuse van Gennep of reductionism. By no means did he try to press all ritual forms into one explanatory framework. He simply noted an underlying pattern in rites that marked a passage from one state to another, without taking away or reducing all the other aspects or ‘individual purposes’ that such rites may also have (ibid.: 11). In other words – and this is important to stress – van Gennep’s work cannot be used directly to argue for any specific theory of rites. Van Gennep detected a pattern, a sequence, a ritual form. The ritual pattern was 10 Marcel Mauss, without here acknowledging Van Gennep’s quite evident influence, would later use the same approach in his attempt to delineate, not the structure of rites, but the very ‘nature of sociology’ (Mauss 1968: 9; see also Introduction to these works of Mauss by Mike Gane 2005). Liminality and the Modern 38 apparently universal: all societies use rites to demarcate transitions, and the forms taken by such rites of transition are comparable. And liminal spaces and moments are key to personal and social development, anywhere in the world. It is via the liminal that persons and groups are taken apart, recomposed and regenerated. Van Gennep was right. 1909–1914: Productive Years and First and Only Academic Position in Switzerland Let us take note: the book that made Arnold van Gennep famous half a century later, Rites of Passage, was written in a period when he was bursting with energy and dedication, amazingly prolific, and wholeheartedly engaged in contributing to the foundations of anthropology and sociology. And yet he could not pass that crucial threshold into French academia. He tried, again and again. In both 1907 and 1909 he applied for a position at Collège de France, but for reasons to be discussed below, he was never selected. From 1908 to 1912 he was technically speaking unemployed, living off his translations. Van Gennep was, all the while, building up a solid curriculum. Although he was still not an academic by profession, he became more and more engaged with his academic project. The final writing of Rites de Passage coincided with his decision to leave his job at the ministry and dedicate himself
